Ranke-1913 Jacobian Lens Probe

This is a first trial of Jacobian-lens probing on uzh-echist-org/Ranke-4B-1913. Anthropic introduced this method in their July 6, 2026 research post "A global workspace in language models". The basic idea is to look for words the model is internally working with, not only words it actually writes. Anthropic calls these word-like internal signals J-space.

What the J-lens does

The point of the J-lens is that the model may think about something without later saying it. For example, if a censored model is asked what happened in Tiananmen Square, it might internally represent words such as tank or protest while the final answer avoids saying them.

That is useful for our historical-contamination question. If a model trained to represent the world as of 1913 internally considers the correct answer to a post-1913 question, that is at least consistent with contamination, even if the model never prints the answer.

There is also a measurement problem. We cannot simply look at a middle layer and read off words from it. Middle layers are not yet in the model's final output format. They may contain information about an answer, but later layers still have to transform that information before it becomes directly readable as a word.

The J-lens tries to account for this. It learns how information at each middle layer usually turns into information near the end of the model. Then, when we inspect a prompt, it asks: given what is present in this middle layer, which words is this information on track to support by the time the model is ready to produce text?

How we trained this lens

We trained this lens on ordinary pretraining-like text, not on the two prompts below. Specifically, we used 256 English Project Gutenberg chunks, each 128 tokens long, from manu/project_gutenberg. The fitting step estimates how changes in each middle layer tend to show up near the end of the model. This gives us a generic way to inspect Ranke's internal states. It is not fine-tuning Ranke, and it is not teaching the model any new facts.

What a J-lens result means

When a token has high J-lens rank at some position and layer, the cautious interpretation is: the model has internally represented something close to that word there. This does not mean the model is about to print that word, and it does not prove that the represented fact is true. It means that word is strongly visible in the model's internal computation according to this lens.

For this first trial, we asked whether the lens gives sensible readouts on two probes: a simple known fact, The capital of France is, and a historically post-1913 category, The countries involved in World War I were. The result is qualitatively encouraging: Paris is strongly visible for the capital prompt. For the World War I prompt, the historically correct country tokens generally rank above the less relevant real-country foils. This is not yet a contamination result, but it suggests that the fitted lens is reading meaningful country/capital information rather than arbitrary tokens.

One caveat is visible in the World War I plot: Germany is already salient from around the word countries, before the full phrase World War I has been processed. Its rank improves further as the model reads World War I. That pattern suggests the model already has a generic country association active, and then the World War I phrase sharpens it toward a historically relevant country. So the Germany signal should not be treated as purely caused by the later phrase alone.

How to read the native plots

Each embedded plot is the repository's native layer x position visualization. Rows are layers, columns are prompt token positions, and each cell shows high-ranking decoded tokens at that position/layer. The pinned chips on the right are the candidate choices; clicking a chip changes the token whose rank heatmap is shown.

The capital plot pins Paris, London, Berlin, and Rome, with the heatmap initially showing Paris. The World War I plot pins the WWI country candidates and real-country foils, with the heatmap initially showing Germany.

Compact best-rank summary

Best rank is the best full-vocabulary rank across selected layers at the final prompt position. It is useful for scanning but optimistic because each candidate gets its best layer.
